summary:
The patient presents with a reduced FRC due to inadequate lung expansion caused by surfactant deficiency leading to formation of hyaline membranes composed primarily of fibrin and cell debris within the alveoli.
The critical issue here involves insufficient surface tension regulation resulting in impaired gas exchange and respiratory distress characteristic of neonatal pulmonary insufficiency known as hyaline membrane disease. To summarize Agent2’s reasoning into two concise sentences focusing only on clinically significant aspects related directly to your query about characterizing hyaline membrane disease of the lungs:

1. Reduced Functional Residual Capacity (FRC), attributed mainly to surfactant deficiency causing poor alveolar recruitment post-exhalation.
2. Formation of intrapulmonary hyaline membranes consisting predominantly of fibrin and cellular material impairs normal breathing mechanics seen specifically in newborns suffering from this disorder. 

These core elements capture essential features without extraneous details while addressing both pathophysiology and diagnostic hallmarks pertinent to understanding HMD based solely upon medical knowledge aligned closely with standard curricula or practice guidelines regarding pediatric respiration issues.
